title: Practice Exam #1 (Cloud Architect)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Cloud Architect - 60 questions

questions.text: You are running a recommendation system on Google Cloud that uses Vertex AI Feature Store to serve real-time features to your application. Your team needs to monitor the system to understand how quickly the application is responding to user requests from end to end. Which metric should you primarily monitor?

options.text: Throughput
options.explanation:
Latency measures the time it takes for your application to respond to requests, which is exactly what you need to understand when monitoring response speed. In the context of Vertex AI Feature Store, this includes the time to retrieve features from the feature store and generate predictions. When users interact with your recommendation system, latency tells you how long they're waiting for results. This metric directly answers the question of "how long is it taking" and helps you identify performance bottlenecks in your real-time serving pipeline. Monitoring latency allows you to set service level objectives and ensure your application meets user experience requirements.

Throughput measures the volume of requests your system can handle per unit of time rather than how long individual requests take. Feature freshness tracks how up-to-date your features are, which matters for data quality but doesn't tell you about response times. Error rate shows the percentage of failed requests, which is important for reliability but doesn't indicate how long successful requests are taking. While these metrics are valuable for overall system health, they don't directly measure application response time.

questions.text: You are reviewing security findings in Security Command Center and notice several alerts that require investigation. What is an appropriate way to use Gemini Cloud Assist in this context?

options.text: Configure Gemini to automatically respond to security incidents on your behalf
options.explanation:
Gemini Cloud Assist in Security Command Center serves as an intelligent assistant that helps you understand and interpret security findings. When you encounter alerts or vulnerabilities, you can ask Gemini to provide explanations about what the findings mean, their severity, and potential implications for your environment. This helps security teams quickly comprehend complex security data without needing to research every finding manually. Gemini can clarify technical details, suggest investigation steps, and help you understand the context of security alerts. The tool acts as an advisor that enhances your decision-making capabilities by making security information more accessible and actionable.

Gemini does not take automated actions like responding to incidents or modifying your security posture without human approval. Configuring it to automatically respond to incidents would bypass critical human judgment in security operations. Similarly, having Gemini modify security policies on its own would create risk since policy changes require careful review and authorization. While Gemini can help analyze findings, it doesn't directly interface with communication systems to escalate issues to your incident response team. These limitations exist because security operations require human oversight and decision-making rather than fully autonomous AI actions.

questions.text: Your application runs on Compute Engine VMs and you need visibility into system performance metrics like memory usage, disk I/O, and application logs. Currently, you can only see basic CPU and network metrics in Cloud Monitoring. What should you do to get comprehensive observability?

options.text: Install the Ops Agent on your Compute Engine VMs
options.explanation:
The Ops Agent is Google's unified agent for Compute Engine that collects both logs and detailed system metrics from virtual machines and sends them to Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring. While some basic metrics like CPU utilization are collected automatically by the hypervisor, the Ops Agent provides comprehensive visibility including memory usage, disk I/O statistics, process-level metrics, and application logs that would otherwise be unavailable. Once installed on your VMs, the agent runs as a system service that continuously collects and forwards telemetry data to Cloud Operations, enabling you to monitor system health, troubleshoot performance issues, and set up alerts based on detailed metrics.

Enabling Cloud Operations at the project level does not automatically install agents on existing VMs or collect the detailed metrics and logs that require agent-based collection from within the operating system. VPC Flow Logs capture network traffic metadata for troubleshooting connectivity issues but do not provide system performance metrics like memory usage or disk I/O, nor do they collect application logs. Deploying a monitoring container using Docker adds unnecessary complexity and does not integrate with the native monitoring and logging services of Cloud Operations, requiring you to build custom solutions for metric collection, storage, and visualization that the Ops Agent provides out of the box.

questions.text: Knightmotives Automotives has a central MySQL database that stores supplier updates for all its manufacturing plants. Each plant currently consumes data from this central database in XML format and saves it to a local FTP server. However, some plants report mismatches between their local data and the data in the headquarters database. Headquarters wants a more controlled and reliable way to ensure every plant’s data remains consistent with the source of truth. What should they do?

options.text: Create a central Pub/Sub topic and configure each plant with a Pull subscription to receive updates from the topic
options.explanation:
This solution ensures all plants are working with consistent and synchronized data that reflects the current state of the source database. Cloud SQL’s managed replication automatically keeps the read replicas up to date while providing each region with low-latency access. Headquarters maintains full control over the data and schema, which eliminates errors caused by exporting, transforming, or manually syncing files. Because replication is handled natively by Cloud SQL, data integrity is maintained and operational complexity is reduced.

Using Pub/Sub with either Pull or Push subscriptions would require converting database updates into messages, which introduces serialization, ordering, and deduplication challenges. That approach is better suited for event-driven processing rather than maintaining synchronized copies of a database. Exporting to Cloud Storage and reimporting XML data would be slow and error-prone, increasing the chance of outdated or inconsistent records. Direct read access to regional replicas provides a simple and reliable way to ensure every plant has accurate and current information.

options.text: Migrate the central MySQL database to Cloud SQL and create read replicas in the regions where each plant operates so each plant reads directly from its regional replica

questions.text: You are building a machine learning model using customer data stored in BigQuery. Some of the columns contain personally identifiable information (PII). Your organization’s compliance team requires that this data remain protected both while stored and while accessed for model training. However, you must ensure that no change to the data affects the accuracy or performance of the model. What should you do?

options.text: Use Sensitive Data Protection to scan the dataset and replace all detected PII with randomly generated placeholder strings before training
options.explanation:
Column-level security controls who can view specific fields without altering or masking the data itself. This ensures that sensitive columns remain protected and encrypted at rest, while the model training process can still use the data in its original form for accuracy. Because the underlying data is not modified, the model’s statistical patterns and performance are preserved. The approach balances security and compliance with the need for accurate, high-quality training data and leverages built-in BigQuery permissions rather than external tools or transformations.

Replacing PII with random strings changes the structure of the data and removes meaningful relationships between features, which can degrade model results. Encrypting fields manually makes them unreadable to the model and prevents useful training on those attributes. Exporting the dataset and dropping columns with PII avoids compliance risks but can eliminate valuable predictive information. Column-level security keeps access limited while allowing accurate and secure model training.

options.text: Use column-level security in BigQuery to restrict access to sensitive fields so only authorized users and the model training service account can read them

questions.text: Your company is developing a healthcare analytics platform that must meet strict data residency requirements. All patient data must be stored and accessed only within Canada, and no data may be transferred or read from any other region. You need to ensure that the application meets these requirements. Select two.

options.text: Use IAM conditions to restrict access to users connecting from Canadian IP ranges
options.explanation:
Organization Policy with resource location constraints enforces storage residency by restricting where resources and datasets can be created, ensuring they remain within allowed regions such as northamerica-northeast1 in Montréal. This control operates at the organization level and blocks attempts to create or move resources outside Canada. VPC Service Controls complement this by creating a service-level perimeter that prevents data from being copied or transferred to projects, APIs, or services outside the defined boundary. Together, they enforce both data location and containment, providing the standard compliance approach for regional data residency.

Firewall rules that block non-Canadian IPs focus only on incoming traffic and do not prevent API-based or internal data transfers between Google Cloud services. IAM conditions limit access based on identity and context but cannot guarantee that stored data stays within Canada. These methods offer partial security but fail to address the full compliance requirement of both where data is stored and how it can move within Google Cloud.

questions.text: Your company manages customer and operations data across several systems, including Salesforce CRM, an external ticketing platform, and an internal inventory management application hosted on Google Cloud. You need to ensure that data is exchanged and transformed consistently between all three systems so that updates in one system are reflected in the others. The solution should handle both integration and transformation across APIs and services. What should you do?

options.text: Use Cloud Workflows to call APIs between Salesforce, the ticketing system, and the internal application to synchronize updates
options.explanation:
Application Integration provides a managed way to orchestrate data movement and transformations between multiple business systems through prebuilt connectors and workflow automation. It can synchronize records across SaaS tools like Salesforce and external ticketing platforms while integrating with internal APIs on Google Cloud. The service supports both trigger-based and event-driven flows, ensuring that updates propagate consistently across all systems. This makes it suitable for enterprise environments where multiple applications need coordinated data exchange without building custom pipelines.

questions.text: Your company is migrating a critical customer-facing database to Google Cloud. The business requirements specify that you must maintain a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 5 minutes and a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 90 seconds to minimize data loss and service disruption. What solution should you implement?

options.text: Deploy Cloud SQL with high availability configuration using a primary instance in one zone and a synchronous standby replica in a different zone within the same region
options.explanation:
Cloud SQL regional high availability uses synchronous replication between zones in the same region, which provides an RPO of essentially zero seconds since data is committed to both the primary and standby before acknowledging writes. The automatic failover process typically completes within 60-120 seconds, meeting the 90-second RTO requirement. This configuration balances cost efficiency with the reliability needed to satisfy both recovery objectives while keeping your database within a single region for lower latency.

The cross-region failover option uses asynchronous replication, which introduces replication lag and cannot guarantee the 5-minute RPO since data loss depends on when the failure occurs. Additionally, cross-region failover typically takes 3-5 minutes or longer, missing the 90-second RTO target. Automated snapshots every 5 minutes might satisfy the RPO requirement, but the restore process takes significantly longer than 90 seconds, often requiring 5-15 minutes or more depending on database size. Cloud Spanner with multi-region configuration would certainly meet both requirements with near-zero RPO and RTO, but it represents significant over-engineering and unnecessary cost for requirements that can be satisfied with standard Cloud SQL regional HA.

questions.text: Your application runs on multiple Compute Engine instances that need concurrent access to a shared file system. The workload requires high-performance I/O operations and the storage capacity needs to scale automatically as your data grows. The instances need to read and write to the same files simultaneously without performance degradation. What storage solution should you use?

options.text: Deploy Filestore Enterprise for the shared file system
options.explanation:
Filestore Enterprise provides a managed NFS file system that multiple Compute Engine instances can mount and access concurrently with high performance. The Enterprise tier automatically scales both performance and capacity as your workload demands increase, which meets your requirement for scaling with capacity. This tier is specifically designed for demanding workloads that need fast I/O operations and can handle the concurrent read/write access patterns from multiple instances. Filestore Enterprise delivers consistent low latency and high throughput that applications expect from a shared file system. The managed nature means you don't have to worry about provisioning capacity in advance or managing performance manually as your needs grow.

Filestore Zonal requires you to manually provision capacity upfront rather than automatically scaling as data grows, which doesn't match the requirement for automatic scaling. Regional persistent disks cannot be attached to multiple instances simultaneously in read-write mode, so they don't support the concurrent access pattern needed here. Multi-region Cloud Storage buckets are object storage rather than file systems and don't provide the file system semantics or I/O performance that applications typically need when they require a shared file system. While you can mount Cloud Storage as a file system using tools like Cloud Storage FUSE, the performance characteristics are not suitable for workloads requiring fast I/O operations.

questions.text: Altostrat Media runs large-scale rendering jobs on Compute Engine. Their workloads can handle interruptions and restarts without issue. The company’s main goal is to reduce infrastructure costs while maintaining acceptable performance. What should they do?

options.text: Use standard Compute Engine VMs with sustained use discounts
options.explanation:
Spot VMs provide low-cost, interruptible compute capacity that is ideal for workloads that can tolerate disruptions. Because Altostrat Media’s rendering jobs are fault-tolerant and can resume if interrupted, Spot VMs deliver significant cost savings compared to standard or committed instances. The company can use autoscaling and job queuing to maintain throughput while minimizing spend, making this option the most efficient way to handle high-compute, restartable workloads.

Sustained use discounts apply to continuously running VMs and do not achieve the same savings as interruptible capacity. Committed use contracts require predictable long-term usage, which conflicts with the flexible, temporary nature of batch rendering jobs. Cloud Run is optimized for stateless, request-based applications rather than persistent, compute-heavy workloads that rely on full machine control. Spot VMs best match the cost and reliability profile for Altostrat Media’s needs.

questions.text: Your organization is migrating a complex application consisting of multiple microservices to Google Cloud. The microservices have numerous interdependencies and communicate with each other through various APIs and messaging systems. You need to ensure there are no service outages during the migration. What should you do?

options.text: Perform a lift-and-shift migration of all microservices during a maintenance window
options.explanation:
Understanding the dependencies between microservices is critical for a successful migration without outages. When you map these dependencies, you can identify which services rely on others and plan a migration sequence that maintains these relationships throughout the process. Testing validates that the microservices work correctly in the new environment and that all the communication paths between them function as expected. This approach lets you catch integration issues, configuration problems, or networking complications before they impact production users. By thoroughly validating the system before cutting over, you reduce the risk of discovering breaking changes only after users are affected. This methodical approach is essential for complex distributed systems where a single misconfigured service can cascade into widespread failures.

Migrating everything in a short timeframe in a maintenance window creates enormous risk because you're changing many components at once without validation, making it difficult to troubleshoot if problems occur. Using Terraform to deploy infrastructure is valuable for consistency, but deploying everything and switching traffic immediately still skips the validation step needed to ensure services work correctly together in the new environment. Migrating frontend services first without understanding dependencies could break the application if those services depend on backends that haven't been migrated yet or can't properly communicate across environments. These approaches all share the same fundamental flaw of not validating that the complex system works correctly before exposing it to production traffic.

questions.text: Your organization is planning to migrate on-premises virtual machines and an SAP instance to Google Cloud. You need to quickly estimate the costs of running these workloads in the cloud before proceeding with the migration. What should you use?

options.text: Use the Google Cloud Pricing Calculator to manually estimate costs
options.explanation:
Migration Center is specifically designed to help you assess your on-premises infrastructure and generate cost estimates for migrating to Google Cloud. It can discover and analyze your existing VMs and applications, including specialized workloads like SAP, and automatically map them to appropriate Google Cloud resources. The tool provides cost projections based on your actual workload characteristics rather than requiring you to manually configure every detail. Migration Center streamlines the estimation process by pulling information directly from your environment, which is much faster than manually entering specifications for each VM and application. This automated assessment is particularly valuable when dealing with complex enterprise applications like SAP that have specific requirements and dependencies.

The Pricing Calculator requires you to manually input all the specifications for each resource, which is time-consuming when you have many VMs and don't provide automated discovery of your existing infrastructure. Cloud Billing reports show costs for resources you're already running on Google Cloud, but they can't help estimate costs for workloads that are still on-premises. Migrate to Virtual Machines is a migration execution tool that helps move VMs to the cloud, but it's designed for performing the actual migration rather than the upfront assessment and cost estimation phase. These tools either require manual effort, only work for existing cloud resources, or focus on execution rather than planning.

questions.text: Your company has deployed a Cloud Run service that needs to connect to an AlloyDB database instance for storing application data. The AlloyDB instance was created with default settings and uses a private IP address within your VPC. When you attempt to connect from Cloud Run using default settings, the connection fails. What should you do to enable connectivity?

options.text: Configure Direct VPC Egress on your Cloud Run service to assign it a private IP address within your VPC network
options.explanation:
Direct VPC Egress is the current recommended approach for connecting Cloud Run to private VPC resources like AlloyDB. By default, Cloud Run services operate outside your VPC on Google's managed network, while AlloyDB instances only have private IP addresses accessible within your VPC. Direct VPC Egress solves this by giving your Cloud Run service a private IP address directly in your VPC, enabling it to communicate with AlloyDB as if it were any other resource in the same network. This approach offers the best performance with no bandwidth limitations and represents the simplest configuration for new deployments.

Serverless VPC Access connectors were the traditional solution but have been superseded by Direct VPC Egress for most use cases. While connectors do work, they introduce additional latency, have a 2 Gbps bandwidth limit per connector, and require managing a separate infrastructure component. Private Service Connect is designed for cross-project or cross-VPC scenarios where you need to share services between different network boundaries, making it unnecessarily complex when your Cloud Run service and AlloyDB instance are in the same project and VPC. Cloud NAT is used for outbound internet access from private resources and does not provide connectivity between Cloud Run and private VPC resources like AlloyDB.

questions.text: Your SRE team needs to monitor custom application metrics exported by your microservices running in GKE. The applications expose metrics in a standard format that your team is already familiar with from previous infrastructure. You want to collect these metrics efficiently without managing additional monitoring infrastructure. What should you do?

options.text: Install the Ops Agent on GKE nodes to scrape application metrics endpoints
options.explanation:
Managed Service for Prometheus provides a fully managed solution for collecting, storing, and querying Prometheus-format metrics from applications running in GKE without requiring you to operate Prometheus infrastructure. When enabled on your cluster, it automatically discovers and scrapes Prometheus metrics endpoints exposed by your pods, scaling collection capacity as your workload grows. The service stores metrics in Cloud Monitoring with long-term retention and provides PromQL query support through both the Cloud Console and Grafana, allowing your team to use familiar Prometheus tooling while benefiting from managed operations, automatic scaling, and integration with other Google Cloud observability features.

The Ops Agent is designed for virtual machine monitoring and does not provide Prometheus metric scraping capabilities for containerized workloads in GKE. Using Cloud Run functions to periodically query application endpoints introduces unnecessary complexity with scheduled invocations, lacks the real-time metric collection that continuous scraping provides, and requires custom code to handle metric parsing and forwarding. Using the Cloud Monitoring custom metrics API requires modifying application code to push metrics rather than using the standard Prometheus pull model, breaking compatibility with existing Prometheus instrumentation libraries and requiring significant application changes.

questions.text: Your company needs to migrate 3 petabytes of archived data from your on-premises data center to Google Cloud Storage. The migration must be completed within 12 weeks to meet a regulatory compliance deadline. What should you do?

options.text: Use gcloud storage with parallel composite uploads to transfer the data
options.explanation:
Transfer Appliance is designed specifically for multi-petabyte migrations when network transfer is impractical or time-constrained. With 3 petabytes of data and a 12-week deadline, achieving reliable network transfer would require sustained high bandwidth for the entire duration with no interruptions or slowdowns. Transfer Appliance provides physical storage devices that you load on-site at full disk speed, then ship to Google where they ingest the data into your Cloud Storage buckets. This approach eliminates network bottlenecks, provides predictable timelines, and can handle the full 3 petabytes using multiple appliances shipped in parallel.

Using gcloud storage with parallel uploads still requires transferring 3 petabytes over the network, which would take months to complete reliably within the 12-week window even under optimal conditions. Storage Transfer Service is excellent for ongoing synchronization and cloud-to-cloud transfers but relies on network bandwidth and cannot realistically move 3 petabytes in such a short timeframe. Setting up Cloud VPN would actually reduce available bandwidth due to encryption overhead while still facing the same fundamental challenge of moving massive amounts of data over the network within the deadline.

questions.text: Your company has deployed a Cloud Run service that is publicly accessible via its default *.run.app URL. You have configured a custom domain for the service. You need to implement security policies on all incoming requests and prevent users from bypassing these policies by directly accessing the default URL. What should you do?

options.text: Configure DNS records to block external access to the default run.app domain
options.explanation:
This solution addresses both security requirements by creating an external HTTPS load balancer with Cloud Armor policies attached to evaluate all incoming requests, and restricting Cloud Run's ingress settings to only accept traffic from internal sources and the load balancer. The ingress restriction prevents direct internet access to the default *.run.app URL, forcing all traffic through the load balancer where Cloud Armor policies are enforced. You would point your custom domain to the load balancer's address, ensuring all requests flow through your security controls.

Configuring DNS records cannot block access to Google-managed *.run.app domains since these DNS entries are controlled by Google, not by your organization. Creating just a serverless NEG and load balancer establishes the routing infrastructure but does not implement Cloud Armor policies or restrict ingress settings, leaving the service vulnerable to bypass through direct URL access. Enabling IAM authentication changes the access model to require credentials rather than implementing content-based security policies, and does not prevent authenticated users from bypassing the load balancer by accessing the default URL directly.

questions.text: Your company is using Vertex AI to train machine learning models with sensitive customer data stored in Cloud Storage buckets. You need to implement controls to prevent data exfiltration while ensuring your ML training pipelines continue to function properly. What should you do?

options.text: Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter that includes ml.googleapis.com and documentai.googleapis.com
options.explanation:
VPC Service Controls creates a security perimeter around Google Cloud services to prevent data exfiltration by controlling which APIs can access resources within the perimeter and blocking unauthorized data transfers outside of it. For Vertex AI workloads, you need to include both the Vertex AI service (aiplatform.googleapis.com) where your training jobs and models run, and Cloud Storage (storage.googleapis.com) where your training data and model artifacts are stored. By placing both services within the same perimeter, you ensure they can communicate with each other while preventing data from being copied to services or projects outside the protected boundary.

The perimeter including ml.googleapis.com refers to the legacy AI Platform API rather than the current Vertex AI service, and documentai.googleapis.com is for Document AI which is unrelated to your ML training pipeline. Organization policies can restrict resource locations and enable logging but do not create the network-level security boundary needed to prevent data exfiltration across service APIs. Private Service Connect is designed for private connectivity to services but does not provide the comprehensive API-level access controls and data exfiltration prevention that VPC Service Controls offers for protecting sensitive data across multiple Google Cloud services.

questions.text: Your company operates a global e-commerce platform with customers across North America, Europe, and Asia. The application requires single-digit millisecond read and write latency for users in all regions, strong consistency for transaction data, and automatic replication across multiple regions. What database solution should you use?

options.text: Deploy Cloud SQL with cross-region read replicas in each geographic region
options.explanation:
Cloud Spanner is designed for applications that require strong consistency, high availability, and horizontal scalability across multiple regions. Its claim to fame is that it almost solves the CAP theorem, which says you can only have 2 of either consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

Spanner's multi-region configuration automatically replicates data across regions and uses synchronous replication with TrueTime to maintain consistency while providing low-latency reads and writes. This makes it ideal for globally distributed applications like e-commerce platforms that need consistent transactions for customers around the world.

Cloud SQL with cross-region replicas provides only asynchronous replication, which cannot ensure consistency for global writes. Firestore in native mode is multi-region capable but is a NoSQL document store, not suited for complex relational transactions. A sharded Cloud SQL deployment requires manual data management and cannot ensure global consistency or transparent failover.

questions.text: Your financial services company must comply with regulations requiring hardware-backed cryptographic key storage and full organizational control over encryption key lifecycle management including creation, rotation, and destruction. What encryption solution should you implement?

options.text: Use Google-managed encryption keys with automatic key rotation enabled
options.explanation:
Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) in Cloud KMS (Key Management Service) provide your organization with direct control over the entire key lifecycle, allowing you to create, rotate, disable, and destroy keys according to your compliance requirements and operational needs. The HSM protection level ensures that all cryptographic operations occur within FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security modules, where keys are generated and stored in tamper-resistant hardware and never exist in software form. This combination meets regulatory requirements for both hardware-backed key storage and organizational control over key management.

Google-managed encryption keys are controlled entirely by Google rather than your organization, removing your ability to manage the key lifecycle or meet requirements for customer-controlled key management. Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) give you control over keys but require you to manage the entire key infrastructure yourself outside of Google Cloud, including secure generation, storage, and rotation without the hardware protection and management features that Cloud KMS provides. Customer-managed keys with software protection level provide lifecycle control but store keys in software rather than hardware security modules, failing to meet the regulatory requirement for hardware-backed cryptographic operations.

questions.text: Your GKE cluster is running application workloads where monitoring shows that pods are consistently using only 20-30% of their requested CPU and memory resources. The over-provisioned resource requests are causing inefficient cluster utilization and increased costs. What should you do to optimize resource usage?

options.text: Enable Horizontal Pod Autoscaler and configure the minimum replica count to be lower than the current number of pods
options.explanation:
Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) in Auto mode analyzes the actual CPU and memory consumption of your pods over time and automatically adjusts the resource requests to match real usage patterns. When pods are consistently underutilized, VPA will lower the resource requests, allowing more pods to be scheduled on the same nodes and improving overall cluster efficiency. Auto mode not only provides recommendations but also implements the changes by evicting and recreating pods with the updated resource specifications, ensuring your workloads run with appropriately sized resource allocations without manual intervention.

Horizontal Pod Autoscaler addresses the number of pod replicas based on metrics like CPU utilization or custom metrics, but it does not modify the resource requests of individual pods, so each pod would still be over-provisioned with the same inefficient resource allocation. Manually reducing resource requests in pod specifications requires you to determine the appropriate values through analysis and testing, then maintain these settings over time as usage patterns change, creating ongoing operational overhead without the adaptive optimization that VPA provides. Cluster autoscaler removes nodes when they are underutilized, but this does not solve the root problem of pods having inflated resource requests that prevent efficient packing in the first place.

questions.text: Your application uses custom Python code and a PostgreSQL database to store customer transaction data. You currently run a machine learning model for fraud detection that was developed in-house. You want to modernize the architecture to rapidly adopt the latest AI capabilities and ensure the system remains agile as new ML technologies emerge. What should you do?

options.text: Migrate customer data to Cloud SQL, use Datastream to replicate into BigQuery, and deploy models using BigQuery ML
options.explanation:
Vertex AI endpoints provide managed, low-latency model serving that decouples the machine learning lifecycle from your application code, allowing data science teams to independently train, validate, and deploy new models without requiring application redeployment. This architectural separation creates the agility needed to quickly adopt new model types, frameworks, and AI capabilities as they become available. Vertex AI serves as Google Cloud's unified ML platform where the latest AI developments are integrated first, including advanced models like Gemini, custom training capabilities, and automated MLOps tooling for monitoring and versioning. For real-time fraud detection that evaluates each transaction as it occurs, Vertex AI endpoints deliver the necessary performance while providing a stable API contract that insulates your application from model changes.

Replicating data to BigQuery and using BigQuery ML creates a strong analytics foundation but does not provide the real-time, low-latency prediction endpoints required for transaction-time fraud detection, as BigQuery ML is optimized for batch predictions on large datasets rather than individual real-time inferences. Using AutoML Tables with batch predictions similarly fails to address the real-time serving requirement and locks you into a specific AutoML framework rather than providing flexibility to adopt new model architectures and AI capabilities. Moving to Cloud Spanner addresses database scalability but does not modernize the ML architecture, and developing models in Workbench still requires you to build your own serving infrastructure.

questions.text: Your team needs to deploy applications and their supporting Google Cloud resources to an existing GKE cluster following infrastructure as code principles. You want to manage both Kubernetes resources and Google Cloud services using a unified declarative approach. What should you do?

options.text: Use Config Sync to deploy Kubernetes resources and Terraform to provision Google Cloud infrastructure
options.explanation:
Config Connector enables you to deploy applications TO an existing GKE cluster (not deploying GKE itself) while simultaneously provisioning the supporting Google Cloud resources those applications need, all through standard Kubernetes manifests. When your application running in GKE requires a Cloud SQL database, Pub/Sub topic, or service account, Config Connector allows you to declare these resources as Kubernetes Custom Resources in the same YAML files as your Deployments and Services. This means deploying your application stack to GKE becomes a single unified operation where kubectl apply creates both the in-cluster Kubernetes resources and the external Google Cloud infrastructure, maintaining true infrastructure as code principles where all dependencies are version-controlled and deployed together.

Config Sync handles synchronizing Kubernetes configurations from Git but does not provision the Google Cloud resources your applications need when deployed to GKE, requiring a separate tool like Terraform to manage infrastructure outside the cluster. Config Management focuses on policy enforcement and configuration distribution across clusters but cannot create the Cloud SQL instances, storage buckets, or other GCP services that applications deployed to GKE typically depend on. Policy Controller with Config Validator provides governance and validation of configurations but does not actually provision resources, meaning it cannot create the supporting infrastructure when you deploy applications to your GKE cluster.

questions.text: Your company is planning to migrate multiple on-premises VM workloads to Google Cloud. You want to minimize migration complexity initially while maintaining the ability to modernize and reorganize workloads into separate projects with proper network isolation in the future. What migration strategy should you follow?

options.text: Set up a Shared VPC host project first and migrate each workload into its own dedicated service project from the start
options.explanation:
This phased approach prioritizes getting workloads successfully migrated to the cloud first by keeping the initial architecture simple with all resources and networking in a single project, reducing the complexity of the migration process itself. Once workloads are running stably in Google Cloud, you can convert the original project into a Shared VPC host project where the VPC and its subnets, firewall rules, and routes remain unchanged, then systematically create service projects and migrate different workloads based on team ownership, security boundaries, or functional requirements. By keeping the network configuration in place when converting to a Shared VPC host, you avoid having to recreate networking infrastructure, allowing workloads to move between projects while maintaining their network connectivity and IP addressing.

Setting up Shared VPC infrastructure first and migrating into separate service projects from the start adds significant architectural complexity during the migration phase when teams are still learning Google Cloud operations and may not yet understand optimal project boundaries. Creating individual VPC networks with peering introduces network management overhead and complexity that works against the goal of simplifying the initial migration, and peering has limitations on the number of connections and transitive routing. Migrating to separate projects with standalone VPCs and then consolidating requires completely rebuilding the network architecture including recreating all VPCs, subnets, firewall rules, and potentially changing IP addresses, which is far more disruptive than the incremental approach of converting the initial project into a host and moving only compute resources into service projects.

options.explanation:

questions.text: You have deployed a new version of your application to Cloud Run and want to gradually roll it out to users by sending 10% of traffic to the new version while keeping 90% on the current version. What should you do?

options.text: Create two separate Cloud Run services and use Cloud Load Balancer to distribute traffic with weighted backend services
options.explanation:
Cloud Run has built-in traffic management capabilities that allow you to split traffic between different revisions of the same service directly through the service configuration. When you deploy a new version, Cloud Run creates a new revision while keeping previous revisions available, and you can configure what percentage of incoming requests should be routed to each revision using tags or percentage-based splits. This traffic splitting happens automatically at the Cloud Run routing layer without requiring external load balancers or DNS configuration, allowing you to perform gradual rollouts, canary deployments, or A/B testing by simply adjusting the traffic percentages in the service settings.

Creating separate Cloud Run services and using Cloud Load Balancer adds unnecessary complexity and cost since Cloud Run already provides native traffic splitting capabilities within a single service, and managing multiple services makes rollback and version management more complicated. Using Cloud DNS with weighted routing policies operates at the DNS level which introduces caching issues and does not provide the fine-grained, immediate control over traffic distribution that Cloud Run's built-in splitting offers. Cloud Armor is a security service for DDoS protection and WAF rules rather than a traffic management tool, and cannot perform percentage-based traffic splitting between different application versions or services.

questions.text: Your data science team needs a collaborative environment where they can develop machine learning models, share notebooks, access company data sources, and work together on experiments within your Google Cloud organization. What should you use?

options.text: Set up Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks with shared access to a common Cloud Storage bucket for collaboration
options.explanation:
Colab Enterprise is specifically designed for team collaboration on machine learning projects within enterprise Google Cloud environments, providing a managed notebook service where multiple users can share notebooks, comment on code, and work together in real time. Unlike the free public Colab, Colab Enterprise integrates directly with your organization's IAM, VPC networks, and data sources like BigQuery and Cloud Storage, ensuring data governance and security controls are maintained. The platform provides built-in version control, the ability to schedule notebook execution, and seamless integration with Vertex AI for training and deployment, creating a complete collaborative workflow without requiring infrastructure management.

Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks are designed for individual data scientists rather than real-time collaboration, and while notebooks can be shared through Cloud Storage, this approach lacks the collaborative editing, commenting, and version control features that teams need for effective joint development. Running JupyterLab on a shared Compute Engine instance requires manual infrastructure management including updates, scaling, and security patching, and does not provide the collaborative features like simultaneous editing and inline comments that modern data science teams expect. Storing notebooks in Cloud Source Repositories supports version control but requires team members to work locally and manually sync changes, missing the integrated cloud compute resources and real-time collaboration capabilities that enable productive teamwork on ML projects.

questions.text: Your company operates a fleet of delivery vehicles, each equipped with multiple IoT sensors that continuously report metrics like speed, fuel level, temperature, and location. You need to store this time-series data in Bigtable to support analytics queries that examine sensor patterns across the entire fleet during specific time windows. What schema design should you use?

options.text: Row key as timestamp followed by vehicle_id, column family as sensor_type, column qualifier as measurement_value
options.explanation:
Bigtable stores data in lexicographically sorted order by row key, which means rows beginning with earlier timestamps will be physically adjacent to rows with slightly later timestamps, enabling efficient range scans across time periods. By placing timestamp first in the row key followed by vehicle_id, all sensor readings from the entire fleet during a specific time window are clustered together in storage, allowing a single sequential read to retrieve fleet-wide data for temporal analysis. The column family serves as a logical grouping with a single family like sensor_data being sufficient for IoT telemetry since all sensors share similar access patterns and storage requirements. Column qualifiers should be the dynamic dimension that varies per reading, and using sensor_type as the qualifier allows each row to flexibly store different sensor measurements without predefined schema constraints, which is ideal since different vehicles might have different sensor configurations over time.

Using sensor_type as the column family instead of a qualifier creates operational overhead since column families must be defined at table creation time and changing them requires schema modifications, making it inflexible when adding new sensor types. Starting the row key with vehicle_id followed by timestamp means lexicographic ordering groups all data by vehicle first, so analyzing fleet-wide patterns at a specific time requires scanning through every vehicle's data sequentially rather than performing a single efficient time-based range scan. Including sensor_type in the row key as timestamp followed by sensor_type creates lexicographic ordering that scatters related vehicle data across multiple row key prefixes, preventing efficient retrieval of all sensor readings for vehicles during a time period since you would need multiple separate scans for each sensor type.

questions.text: Your company operates a fleet of 50,000 delivery vehicles, each equipped with multiple IoT sensors that continuously report metrics like speed, fuel level, temperature, and location every few seconds. You need to store this high-volume time-series data in Bigtable with a schema design that supports efficient reads and writes at scale while enabling analytics queries that examine sensor patterns across the entire fleet during specific time windows. What schema design should you use?

options.text: Row key as vehicle_id followed by timestamp, column family as sensor_data, column qualifier as sensor_type
options.explanation:
Bigtable stores data in lexicographically sorted order by row key and distributes row key ranges across multiple tablet servers for horizontal scaling. Starting the row key with vehicle_id ensures writes are naturally distributed across many tablets since each of the 50,000 vehicles generates data independently, preventing write hotspots that would occur if all recent data targeted the same row key prefix. Within each vehicle's data, the timestamp suffix maintains chronological ordering for efficient time-range queries on individual vehicles. While analyzing patterns across the entire fleet during specific time windows requires querying multiple vehicle ranges rather than a single scan, this can be achieved efficiently through parallel queries or batch processing pipelines that aggregate data from multiple vehicles, making the tradeoff worthwhile to maintain high write throughput across the massive volume of incoming sensor data.

Starting the row key with timestamp followed by vehicle_id creates severe write hotspots because all incoming sensor data for the current time period from 50,000 vehicles gets written to the same narrow row key range, overloading a single tablet server and creating a bottleneck that limits write throughput regardless of how many servers are in the cluster. Using vehicle_type as the first component creates hotspots with low-cardinality categorical data, since all vehicles of the same type write to the same tablet, limiting write distribution to only as many tablets as you have vehicle types rather than distributing across thousands of unique vehicle identifiers. Placing timestamp first followed by vehicle_type combines both problems by creating temporal write hotspots where all current data targets one row key range while also clustering data by vehicle type, severely limiting write scalability and preventing Bigtable from effectively distributing the high-volume workload across its cluster.

questions.text: Your development team has deployed applications to a GKE Standard cluster, but when investigating performance issues, they discover that no logs or metrics are visible in Cloud Logging or Cloud Monitoring. options.text: Install the Ops Agent on each GKE node using a DaemonSet
options.explanation:
GKE provides native integration with Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring through cluster-level settings that can be enabled on existing clusters without installing node-level agents or modifying workloads. Once enabled, the platform automatically collects system logs, workload logs, and metrics from nodes and pods and sends them to the appropriate Cloud Operations services. This built-in integration provides unified visibility into cluster health, resource utilization, and application behavior across all workloads in the cluster without requiring manual agent deployment or infrastructure changes.

The Ops Agent is designed for Compute Engine virtual machines rather than GKE clusters, and installing it manually on nodes is unnecessary because GKE's observability is managed at the control plane level through the Cloud Operations integration. Configuring kubectl to export logs and metrics to Cloud Storage requires manual scripting and does not provide real-time monitoring or the structured log analysis capabilities that Cloud Logging offers, nor does it integrate with Cloud Monitoring's alerting and dashboard features. Deploying logging sidecar containers in each pod only addresses log collection and requires modifying every deployment, while missing the comprehensive metrics collection that Cloud Monitoring automatically performs once the cluster-level observability features are enabled.

questions.text: Your team needs to implement a CI/CD pipeline for a containerized application that will be deployed to multiple GKE environments including development, staging, and production clusters. You want to automate both the container image creation and the progressive deployment across environments with approval gates between stages. What should you implement?

options.text: Configure Cloud Build to build container images, push to Artifact Registry, and trigger Cloud Deploy pipelines to promote releases through dev, staging, and production with approval gates
options.explanation:
Cloud Build specializes in continuous integration tasks like building container images from source code, running tests, and pushing artifacts to registries, while Cloud Deploy focuses specifically on continuous delivery by managing the deployment pipeline across multiple target environments. By combining both services, Cloud Build handles the build and test phases to produce verified container images in Artifact Registry, then Cloud Deploy orchestrates the progressive rollout across your development, staging, and production clusters with built-in support for approval workflows, canary deployments, and rollback capabilities. This separation of concerns allows each service to excel at its specific function while providing an integrated pipeline that handles the complete CI/CD workflow with proper stage gates and promotion policies.

Having Cloud Build trigger Cloud Deploy pipelines directly creates unnecessary coupling between the CI and CD phases, when Cloud Deploy is designed to watch for new releases in Artifact Registry and automatically initiate delivery pipelines independently. Using separate Cloud Build triggers with manual approvals for each environment requires custom scripting to manage deployment orchestration, approval gates, and progressive delivery patterns that Cloud Deploy provides natively, increasing complexity and maintenance burden. Cloud Deploy does not build container images or pull source code since it is designed to deploy pre-built artifacts from registries rather than perform compilation or image creation tasks that belong in the CI phase.

questions.text: Your team is implementing a CI/CD pipeline for a microservices application that deploys through development, staging, and production environments. You need to determine the appropriate testing strategy at each stage to balance feedback speed, resource costs, and deployment confidence. What testing approach should you implement?

options.text: Use Cloud Build to run unit tests and integration tests, run load tests in development, run smoke tests in staging, and run acceptance tests in production
options.explanation:
This testing strategy optimizes for fast feedback cycles and appropriate validation at each stage based on the risk and cost profile of different test types. Unit tests run quickly and validate individual component logic without requiring deployed infrastructure, making them ideal for Cloud Build CI pipelines where they can gate code merges and provide immediate feedback to developers. Integration and load tests require full system infrastructure and validate that components work together correctly under expected load, making the development environment appropriate where complete resources are available for thorough testing without impacting production-like environments. Smoke tests in staging provide quick validation that core functionality works in a production-like environment after deployment, while acceptance tests in production verify that the system meets business requirements with real configurations and user traffic patterns.

Running integration tests in Cloud Build is impractical since they require deployed infrastructure with databases, message queues, and other services that are not available in the CI build environment, making them better suited for deployed development environments. Running unit tests only locally without CI validation misses the opportunity to enforce quality gates in a consistent build environment and allows untested code to reach shared branches. Running all test types in Cloud Build before any deployment creates extremely long build times and requires provisioning full application infrastructure within the CI pipeline, which is resource-intensive and defeats the purpose of having separate testing environments for different validation stages.

questions.text: Your company runs critical VMware workloads on-premises and needs to implement a disaster recovery solution to Google Cloud that meets a 4-hour RPO and 8-hour RTO requirement. You want a managed solution that minimizes operational complexity. What should you implement?

options.text: Configure Migrate to Virtual Machines to continuously replicate on-premises VMs to Compute Engine with automated failover scripts
options.explanation:
Backup and DR (Disaster Recovery) is specifically designed for disaster recovery scenarios and provides continuous replication of on-premises VMware workloads to Google Cloud with built-in recovery orchestration capabilities. The service handles ongoing replication to meet RPO requirements, maintains recovery points, and provides automated failover workflows that can restore entire application stacks in the proper sequence to meet RTO targets. This managed solution includes features like non-disruptive DR testing, recovery plan management, and monitoring without requiring custom scripts or manual coordination, making it the appropriate choice for enterprise DR requirements.

Migrate to Virtual Machines is designed for one-time migrations rather than ongoing disaster recovery, and while it can replicate VMs, it lacks the continuous replication, recovery orchestration, and DR-specific features like automated failover testing that are essential for meeting defined RPO and RTO targets. Using scheduled snapshots with gsutil transfers creates a manual process that requires custom scripting for replication, recovery orchestration, and failover procedures, increasing operational complexity and making it difficult to reliably meet recovery time objectives during actual disaster scenarios. Persistent Disk snapshots are designed for Compute Engine VMs already running in Google Cloud rather than on-premises workloads, and building a custom replication solution requires significant engineering effort to handle continuous replication, consistency groups, and recovery automation that Backup and DR provides as a managed service.

questions.text: Your data science team needs to access large datasets stored in Cloud Storage buckets from their machine learning training jobs running on Compute Engine VMs. The workloads primarily perform sequential reads of data files and do not require file locking or complex file system operations. What solution should you implement to provide file system access to the Cloud Storage data?

options.text: Create a Filestore Basic instance and copy all dataset files from Cloud Storage to the NFS share before training
options.explanation:
Cloud Storage FUSE allows you to mount Cloud Storage buckets as a file system on Linux instances, enabling applications to access object storage using standard file system operations without requiring data migration or duplication. For read-heavy workloads like machine learning training that primarily perform sequential reads of large files, Cloud Storage FUSE provides adequate performance while keeping data in its native Cloud Storage location where it benefits from object storage's scalability, durability, and cost-effectiveness. This approach eliminates the need to copy or sync data to separate file systems, reducing storage costs and operational complexity while maintaining a single source of truth in Cloud Storage.

Creating a Filestore instance and copying datasets introduces unnecessary data duplication, storage costs, and synchronization complexity when the data already exists in Cloud Storage and the workload does not require high-performance random access patterns. Running a custom NFS server that syncs from Cloud Storage adds operational overhead for managing the server infrastructure and requires custom scripting to handle data synchronization, updates, and consistency. Deploying Filestore Enterprise with scheduled replication is excessive for a use case that does not require the high availability features or low-latency random access that justifies the enterprise tier, and still involves unnecessary data duplication from Cloud Storage.

questions.text:
Your application serves API requests, static web assets, and video content to users globally. You need to optimize the architecture to provide the best user experience with low latency for all content types.

Select TWO.

options.text: Store videos and static assets in a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket
options.explanation:
Cloud CDN caches static assets and video content at Google's globally distributed edge locations, dramatically reducing latency by serving content from points of presence near users rather than requiring requests to travel back to origin servers for every access. A global external HTTP(S) load balancer intelligently routes API requests to the nearest healthy backend across multiple regions, ensuring low latency for dynamic API responses that cannot be cached. Together, these solutions address both the caching needs for static content delivery and the intelligent geographic routing needs for dynamic API traffic, providing comprehensive global performance optimization across all application components.

Storing videos in multi-region buckets provides geographic distribution of origin data but does not deliver content from edge locations near users, so requests still experience higher latency compared to CDN caching at hundreds of edge points of presence. Configuring Cloud Armor adds security protections like DDoS mitigation and WAF rules but does not improve content delivery performance or reduce latency for users accessing videos, static assets, or APIs. Storing content in a single-region bucket creates a performance bottleneck where all requests must reach back to one geographic location, significantly increasing latency for global users and eliminating the benefits of geographic distribution.

questions.text: Your company stores audit logs and transaction records in Cloud Storage that are accessed frequently during the first 120 days and rarely thereafter. Regulations require retaining these files for a minimum of 3 years before deletion. You want to optimize storage costs while maintaining compliance and operational flexibility. What should you do?

options.text: Configure object lifecycle management rules to transition objects to Coldline after 120 days and delete after 3 years
options.explanation:
Object lifecycle management provides declarative rules that automatically transition objects between storage classes based on age and delete them after specified retention periods, matching the access pattern and compliance requirements described. By configuring a rule to move objects to Coldline storage after 120 days, you reduce storage costs for infrequently accessed data while keeping it readily available if needed, and setting a deletion rule at 3 years ensures automatic compliance with retention requirements without manual intervention. This approach maintains operational flexibility since lifecycle rules can be easily modified as requirements change, and the automated nature eliminates operational overhead while providing predictable cost optimization.

Autoclass automatically manages storage class transitions based on actual access patterns rather than time-based rules, which means objects would only move to cheaper storage classes after Cloud Storage observes infrequent access, potentially delaying cost savings and not providing the deterministic 120-day transition the scenario requires. Object versioning addresses maintaining multiple versions of files when they are modified but does not provide cost optimization for the primary object lifecycle or automatic deletion after retention periods, and using Nearline for archived versions does not match the primary use case of transitioning all objects based on age. Using Cloud Scheduler with custom scripts introduces operational complexity and requires maintaining custom code for what lifecycle management handles natively, and manual scripting increases the risk of errors in compliance-critical retention policies.

questions.text: Your organization plans to migrate its existing Windows Server workloads from its on-premises VMware environment to Google Cloud. The Windows Server instances have existing licenses that need to be brought over to the cloud environment using BYOL. What should you do to ensure the licenses are properly migrated and the servers operate as expected in Google Cloud?

options.text: Create an image of the on-premises Windows Server virtual machines and import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine using sole-tenancy nodes
options.explanation:
Migrate to Virtual Machines is specifically designed for migrating on-premises workloads to Google Cloud and provides automated handling of Windows Server BYOL licensing scenarios. The service discovers your source VMs, performs continuous replication with minimal performance impact on production systems, and orchestrates the cutover process while maintaining proper license configurations. This approach eliminates manual image creation, reduces migration complexity, and ensures that licensing terms are correctly applied during the migration process, allowing your Windows Server instances to operate compliantly in Google Cloud.

Manually creating images and importing them to sole-tenancy nodes introduces operational complexity and requires manual configuration of licensing parameters that Migrate to Virtual Machines handles automatically, increasing the risk of licensing misconfigurations. Manually reinstalling Windows Server on new instances and transferring data separately is extremely time-consuming, error-prone, and does not preserve the complete system state including configurations and installed applications. Using gcloud commands with BYOL images requires you to manually provision infrastructure and restore application data separately rather than migrating the complete running system, and does not provide the orchestration and validation features that ensure successful migration outcomes.

questions.text: Your team manages a fleet of Compute Engine instances running production workloads. You want to ensure that operating system security patches and updates are applied automatically without manual intervention or custom scripting. What should you do?

options.text: Enable Patch on your Compute Engine instances
options.explanation:
Patch is a built-in Compute Engine feature that automates the process of applying operating system security patches and updates across your VM instances. When enabled, the service automatically schedules patch deployments, handles pre-patch and post-patch operations, and can be configured with maintenance windows to minimize disruption to production workloads. This managed solution eliminates the need for custom automation scripts or manual patching procedures while providing centralized visibility and reporting on patch compliance across your entire fleet of instances.

VM Manager is actually the service that provides OS patch management capabilities in Compute Engine, but simply configuring VM Manager without explicitly enabling the OS patch management feature does not automatically set up patch deployments. Configuring automatic updates within the OS itself handles patching at the individual instance level but requires manual configuration on each instance, does not provide centralized management or reporting, and depends on the instance's own update mechanisms rather than Google Cloud's managed service. Cloud Deployment Manager is an infrastructure as code tool for provisioning and managing Google Cloud resources but does not have native patch deployment scheduling capabilities, and attempting to use it for OS patch automation would require significant custom scripting.

questions.text: Your company needs to grant a third-party vendor access to specific Cloud Storage buckets for a week-long data analytics project. The vendor's access should automatically expire after 7 days without requiring manual revocation, and you need to maintain an audit trail showing which vendor personnel accessed the data. The vendor will access the buckets using their company Google Workspace accounts. What should you do?

options.text: Generate signed URLs for the Cloud Storage objects with a 7-day expiration time and provide them to the vendor
options.explanation:
IAM Conditions allow you to add temporal constraints to role bindings that automatically enforce access expiration without requiring manual intervention or additional automation infrastructure. By adding a time-based condition to the IAM policy binding, you specify an exact timestamp after which the permission automatically becomes invalid, ensuring the vendor's access terminates precisely at the end of the 7-day period. questions.text: Your data science team needs to train a large language model using a massive dataset that requires distributed training across multiple accelerators. The training job is expected to run for several days and requires high-performance interconnect between compute nodes to minimize communication overhead during gradient synchronization. You want to optimize for training performance while managing costs effectively. What should you do?

options.text: Use Vertex AI custom training with GPU clusters and configure multi-node distributed training through the training service
options.explanation:
AI Hypercomputer provides an integrated stack specifically designed for large-scale AI model training, combining TPU pods with high-bandwidth interconnect that minimizes communication latency during distributed training operations. TPU pods offer tightly coupled accelerators connected through Google's custom interconnect fabric, which is critical for efficient gradient synchronization across multiple devices when training large models over extended periods. This purpose-built infrastructure delivers superior performance for multi-day training jobs compared to solutions that rely on standard networking between compute resources.

Vertex AI custom training with GPU clusters uses standard GPU instances connected through regular networking rather than specialized high-bandwidth interconnect, resulting in higher communication overhead. Deploying GKE with GPU node pools and orchestration frameworks offers flexibility but introduces operational complexity and lacks optimized accelerator-to-accelerator connectivity. Using Compute Engine instance groups with VPC peering still relies on standard network infrastructure between GPUs rather than custom interconnect fabric designed for minimizing latency in distributed AI training scenarios.

questions.text: Your company is migrating VMware workloads to Google Cloud and needs to establish connectivity between your existing on-premises VMware infrastructure and Google Cloud VMware Engine for a phased migration approach. The solution must provide low-latency private connectivity and allow seamless integration with other Google Cloud services. What network configuration should you implement?

options.text: Set up a Cloud VPN tunnel between your on-premises environment and Google Cloud VMware Engine for secure encrypted connectivity
options.explanation:
Google Cloud VMware Engine uses Private Service Connect to establish connectivity between the VMware private cloud and your VPC network, providing private IP connectivity without exposing traffic to the public internet. Once the VMware Engine environment is connected to your VPC through Private Service Connect, you can use standard networking options like Cloud Interconnect or Cloud VPN to connect your on-premises environment to that VPC, creating an end-to-end private network path. This architecture also enables your VMware workloads to seamlessly access other Google Cloud services within the VPC, supporting hybrid cloud scenarios during migration.

Cloud VPN cannot connect directly to Google Cloud VMware Engine since the VMware private cloud operates outside the standard VPC networking model and requires Private Service Connect as the integration point. Cloud Interconnect VLAN attachments connect to VPC networks but cannot attach directly to VMware Engine infrastructure, as the service uses a different networking architecture. Shared VPC and standard VPC peering do not apply to Google Cloud VMware Engine since it is not a standard VPC resource and instead requires Private Service Connect for network integration.

questions.text: Your finance company is developing a stock price prediction system that combines historical trading data with news sentiment analysis. The predictions are displayed in a real-time dashboard for traders and analysts. Due to market volatility and evolving economic conditions, the model needs to be retrained and redeployed quarterly. You want to implement an automated, repeatable pipeline that handles the complete ML workflow from data preparation through model deployment. What architecture should you design?

options.text: Use Dataflow for data preprocessing and Cloud Composer to orchestrate the workflow from training to deployment
options.explanation:
Vertex AI Pipelines provides an integrated solution for orchestrating complex machine learning workflows, offering native components for data preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and deployment within a single managed platform. The service enables you to define the complete ML workflow as code, automatically handles dependency management between pipeline steps, and provides monitoring and versioning for reproducible model updates. This integrated approach ensures that quarterly retraining cycles are automated, auditable, and consistent, reducing operational overhead compared to coordinating multiple separate services for each stage of the ML lifecycle.

Using Dataflow with Cloud Composer requires coordinating multiple services and building custom integration logic between data processing and ML operations, increasing complexity compared to Vertex AI Pipelines' native ML workflow components. Triggering model training through BigQuery scheduled queries and Cloud Run Functions creates a fragmented architecture where the ML workflow logic is distributed across multiple services without centralized orchestration or built-in ML-specific features like experiment tracking and model versioning. Cloud Build pipelines are designed for CI/CD of application code rather than ML workflows and lack the specialized components for model training, evaluation, and deployment that Vertex AI Pipelines provides, requiring significant custom development to replicate ML pipeline functionality.

questions.text: Your retail company is building a visual search feature on Vertex AI that allows customers to upload product photos and find similar items in your catalog. You need to select the appropriate pre-built AI capability within Vertex AI to analyze uploaded images and extract features for product matching. What should you use?

options.text: Use Gemini multimodal models in Vertex AI to analyze images and generate product descriptions
options.explanation:
Vision AI provides pre-built image analysis capabilities that can be integrated into Vertex AI workflows for extracting structured information from images such as object detection, labels, and visual features needed for similarity matching. When integrated with Vertex AI, Vision AI enables you to build end-to-end ML pipelines that combine image analysis with custom models or other AI services within a unified platform. This integration approach allows you to leverage pre-built computer vision capabilities without training custom models while maintaining the flexibility to customize the overall solution within the Vertex AI ecosystem.

Gemini multimodal models in Vertex AI are designed for conversational AI and content generation tasks rather than structured feature extraction optimized for visual search applications, and while they can describe images, they don't provide the specialized computer vision outputs needed for efficient product matching. Imagen on Vertex AI is focused on generating new images from text prompts rather than analyzing existing images to extract searchable attributes and features. Video Intelligence API is optimized for video content analysis including temporal patterns and scene detection rather than static image feature extraction that visual search requires.

questions.text: Your customer support team receives thousands of technical support tickets monthly that reference various product issues described in your internal knowledge base. You need a solution that analyzes support tickets against documentation to identify root causes, then automatically creates structured bug reports and routes them to the appropriate engineering teams based on product area and severity. What should you implement?

options.text: Use NotebookLM to analyze support tickets and documentation, then manually create and route bug reports based on the generated insights
options.explanation:
NotebookLM excels at analyzing documents and generating grounded insights from your knowledge base, making it ideal for identifying patterns and root causes across support tickets by comparing them against product documentation. However, NotebookLM focuses on analysis and knowledge synthesis rather than workflow automation, which is where Gemini Enterprise AI Agents provide complementary capabilities by executing multi-step processes like creating bug reports in your ticketing system, determining appropriate routing based on product areas, and updating team workflows. This combination leverages each tool's strengths: NotebookLM for deep document understanding and insight generation, and AI Agents for automating the downstream actions that result from those insights.

Using NotebookLM alone for analysis but relying on manual bug report creation eliminates the automation benefit and creates operational bottlenecks that prevent scaling the support workflow efficiently. Gemini Enterprise AI Agents can handle workflow automation but are not specifically optimized for the deep document analysis and knowledge synthesis tasks that NotebookLM provides when comparing large volumes of tickets against technical documentation. Vertex AI Search with Cloud Run Functions requires custom development to replicate the document understanding capabilities that NotebookLM provides natively, and lacks the sophisticated workflow orchestration and enterprise system integration that AI Agents offer for automating multi-step business processes.
